chemicals.concentration_notes: Extremely potent; present in all parts including pollen and vase water.
chemicals.description: Water-soluble toxin that targets renal tubular epithelial cells, causing acute kidney failure.
chemicals.name: Unidentified Nephrotoxin

description: Daylily (Hemerocallis spp.) is one of the most dangerous plants for cats and must be treated as a veterinary emergency. Although not a true lily botanically, Daylilies share the same devastating nephrotoxicity seen in Easter and Tiger lilies. The specific toxic compound has not been fully identified, but virtually all parts — flowers, leaves, stems, pollen, and even vase water — can cause fatal acute kidney failure.
name: Daylily
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: unknown nephrotoxic substance unique to Hemerocallis; not toxic to dogs or humans
Mechanism: acute tubular necrosis — rapid, irreversible destruction of kidney tissue
Symptoms Phase 1 (0-2h): vomiting, lethargy, drooling; Phase 2 (24-72h): acute kidney failure with cessation of urine output, seizures, coma
Even small exposures are dangerous: a few petals, licking pollen off fur, or drinking vase water can be lethal
Time is critical — decontamination and IV fluid therapy within 6 hours significantly improve survival; do not wait for symptoms to worsen

symptoms.name: Vomiting
symptoms.notes: Early sign (1-3 hours); may subside temporarily.

treatments.description: Aggressive emesis and activated charcoal if early.
treatments.name: Decontamination
treatments.notes: Must be done within hours of ingestion.
